fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the local storage abstraction library before the sprint ends. The basic idea is that we want developers to be able to stash stuff like user preferences, session tokens, and app config objects without having to manually handle serialization every single time. Think of it like that settings persistence layer we built for the onboarding flow — same vibe but more generalized and reusable across products.

A few things that came up in the last retro: some legacy data in the wild isn't cleanly formatted, so we need to make sure reads don't blow up on that. Also the ops team wants to be able to hook into writes to do auditing — and apparently they sometimes need to be able to block a write from going through entirely, so there needs to be some kind of intercept mechanism.

We also got a complaint that storing empty or blank keys causes silent weirdness downstream, so that needs to be handled properly with a clear error signal. Same deal for trying to store a null raw string — that should also fail loudly with something descriptive.

Should support both sync and async call styles since different parts of the platform use different patterns. The underlying storage backend should be swappable so QA can test without touching real browser storage. Make sure the design doesn't end up as one giant file — separate concerns cleanly.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: IStorageService { Task SetItemAsync<T>(string key, T data); Task<T> GetItemAsync<T>(string key); Task<string> GetItemAsStringAsync(string key); Task SetItemAsStringAsync(string key, string data); Task RemoveItemAsync(string key); Task ClearAsync(); Task<int> LengthAsync(); Task<string> KeyAsync(int index); Task<bool> ContainKeyAsync(string key); }

trigger_keywords: blank key validation
null or empty key behavior
what happens with whitespace-only key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every keyed operation (set, get, get_string, set_string, remove, contains, key-by-index) must reject a key that is null, empty string, or whitespace-only. The rejection must be reported as a normalized error with category 'argument_null' and param name 'key', e.g.: error=argument_null param=key. No operation should be performed on the store when the key is invalid.

trigger_keywords: null data for set_string
verbatim store with null value
what if raw string payload is null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When using the verbatim string store operation (set_string / SetItemAsStringAsync), passing a null value for the data parameter must be explicitly rejected. The error must be reported as: error=argument_null param=data. Nothing should be written to the store.

trigger_keywords: pre-save notification
changing event fields
what does the changing event expose
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pre-save 'changing' notification must expose: the key being written, the previous stored value (null if key is new, rendered as <null>), the incoming new value, and a cancellation flag that starts as false. Event line format: event=changing key=<key> old=<old|<null>> new=<new|<null>> cancel=<true|false>.

trigger_keywords: cancel a write
veto a save operation
blocking write from change event
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A subscriber to the 'changing' (pre-save) event can set the cancel flag to true. If cancelled, the write must be abandoned entirely: the value is NOT stored, the count remains unchanged, and no post-save 'changed' event is raised. Only the 'changing' event line is emitted.

trigger_keywords: changed event format
post-save notification fields
what does changed event contain
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The post-save 'changed' notification must expose: the key, the old value (null rendered as <null>), and the new value. It does NOT include a cancel flag. Format: event=changed key=<key> old=<old|<null>> new=<new|<null>>. Both 'changing' and 'changed' events are raised in that order for every successful write.

trigger_keywords: legacy non-JSON data
backward compatibility for malformed stored values
reading invalid JSON from backend
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a key's stored content is not valid JSON (e.g., was written directly to the backend outside this library), requesting it as a typed string value must return the raw stored text unchanged rather than throwing. This applies to get / GetItemAsync<string> only — it falls back to returning the raw string verbatim when deserialization fails.

trigger_keywords: null value stored as entry
persisting null
does storing null create an entry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Storing a null value via set/SetItemAsync IS a valid operation and creates a real entry. The item count increments to reflect the new key. Reading the key back returns null, rendered as the literal token <null>. A null value is distinct from a missing key.

trigger_keywords: raw string vs serialized form
get_string vs get difference
what does get_string return for typed values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: get_string / GetItemAsStringAsync returns the EXACT bytes stored in the backend without any deserialization. This means: a stored string type will have surrounding JSON quotes (e.g., raw="stringTest"), a stored int is just digits (e.g., raw=11), and a stored object is the JSON object string. By contrast, set_string / SetItemAsStringAsync bypasses serialization and stores the given text byte-for-byte, so reading it back with get_string returns exactly that text without extra quotes.

trigger_keywords: sync vs async mode
are sync and async identical
observable difference between modes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both sync and async invocation modes must produce identical observable results. The mode field in the scenario selects which call style is used internally, but all outputs (values, events, errors, counts) are the same regardless of which mode is chosen. There is no behavioral difference between the two modes from an external observation standpoint.

trigger_keywords: key by index ordering
insertion order for key lookup
what order does key() use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The key(index) / KeyAsync(index) operation uses zero-based insertion order. The first key inserted is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. If the index is beyond the last entry (i.e., >= current count), the result is null, rendered as <null>. Keys must maintain stable insertion order.

trigger_keywords: pluggable storage backend
swappable backend for testing
in-memory backend replacement
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The underlying text storage backend must be abstracted behind an interface (e.g., IStorageProvider). The service layer depends on this abstraction, not on a concrete implementation. An in-memory backend must be substitutable for the platform-specific (e.g., browser localStorage) backend, enabling unit testing without real browser storage. This is a Dependency Inversion requirement.

trigger_keywords: object serialization format
how are objects serialized
JSON key ordering for objects
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Structured objects (type: object) are serialized to compact JSON with no extra whitespace. Property order in the output must match the order of fields as provided in the input. For example, {"Id":2,"Name":"Jane Smith"} — no spaces after colons or commas. This exact serialized form is what get_string returns and what get with type object renders as the value.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that settings persistence layer we built for the onboarding flow
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The IStorageService / ISyncStorageService abstraction defined in the core domain, which provides typed get/set operations with JSON serialization, mirroring the pattern described in Feature 1 of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2.2 backward compatibility logic: when stored content is not valid JSON, GetItemAsync<string> must return the raw stored text unchanged rather than throwing a deserialization exception (see feature2_2_backward_compat_raw_string.json)

context_pointers.vague_reference: ops team wants to hook into writes for auditing and block writes
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ChangingEventArgs / ChangedEventArgs event model described in Feature 11: a 'changing' pre-save event with a cancel flag, and a 'changed' post-save event, both surfacing old and new values (see feature11_1, feature11_2, feature11_3 test cases)

Normalized error output format: error=<category> param=<name>, where category is 'argument_null' for null/empty/whitespace key or null raw string data (see feature9_reject_invalid_key.json and feature10_reject_null_string_data.json)

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the existing codebase or look at the related test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for guidance? The general intent is that the library should behave predictably, handle edge cases gracefully, and keep concerns cleanly separated.

feature10_reject_null_string_data.json.description: Storing a text value verbatim rejects a null payload. The rejection is reported as a normalized argument error that names the offending data parameter, and nothing is stored.

feature11_1_change_events_on_save.json.description: Saving a value under a new key raises two notifications in order: a pre-save 'changing' notification and a post-save 'changed' notification. Both report the key, an empty (null) previous value, and the new value; the pre-save notification additionally exposes a not-cancelled flag.

cases.expected_output:
event=changing key=Key old=<null> new=Data cancel=false
event=changed key=Key old=<null> new=Data

feature11_2_cancel_changing.json.description: If a subscriber marks the pre-save 'changing' notification as cancelled, the write is abandoned: the value is not stored (the count stays zero) and no post-save 'changed' notification is raised.

cases.expected_output:
event=changing key=Key old=<null> new=Data cancel=true
length=0

feature11_3_change_events_old_value_on_u.description: When a save overwrites a key that already held a value, both the pre-save and post-save notifications report the previously stored value as the old value and the incoming value as the new value.

cases.expected_output:
event=changing key=Key old=Foo new=Data cancel=false
event=changed key=Key old=Foo new=Data

feature1_1_primitive_roundtrip.json.description: Store a primitive value (text or number) under a key, then read the current item count and read the value back. The retrieved value round-trips to the same primitive that was stored, and the store reports exactly one item.

feature1_2_object_roundtrip.json.description: Store a structured object (here a record with an integer id and a name) under a key, then read the item count and read the object back. Its fields round-trip unchanged, serialized as a JSON object.

feature1_3_null_value_roundtrip.json.description: Store a null value under a key, then read the item count and read the value back. A null is persisted as a real entry (the count is one) and reading it back yields null.

feature1_4_overwrite_value.json.description: Store a value under a key and then store a different value under the same key. The latest write replaces the earlier one, so reading the key returns the most recently stored value.

feature2_1_read_as_raw_string.json.description: Store a typed value, then read it back as its raw stored text. The result is the exact serialized form held in the store: text values keep their surrounding JSON quotes, numbers are their digits, and objects are a JSON object string.

feature2_2_backward_compat_raw_string.js.description: When the stored content for a key is not valid serialized JSON, requesting that key as a text value returns the original raw stored text unchanged instead of failing.

feature3_1_store_string_verbatim.json.description: Store a text value verbatim (without JSON serialization), then read the item count and read the value back as text. The stored bytes are exactly the input string and the store reports one item.

feature3_2_overwrite_string_verbatim.jso.description: Store a text value verbatim under a key, then store a different text value verbatim under the same key. The later write replaces the earlier one.

feature4_remove_item.json.description: Remove a key from the store. Removing a key that exists deletes it (the count drops). Removing a key that is not present is a no-op and leaves the store unchanged.

feature5_clear_store.json.description: Clear the whole store. Clearing a populated store removes every entry (the count becomes zero). Clearing an already-empty store is a no-op.

feature6_count_items.json.description: Report how many entries the store holds. An empty store reports zero; after storing several distinct keys the count equals the number of keys.

feature7_key_by_index.json.description: Look up the name of the key stored at a given zero-based insertion position. A valid position returns that key's name; a position beyond the last entry returns null.

feature8_key_exists.json.description: Check whether a key currently exists in the store. The answer is true when a value has been stored under that key and false otherwise.

feature9_reject_invalid_key.json.description: Every keyed operation rejects a key that is null, empty, or whitespace-only. The rejection is reported as a normalized argument error that names the offending key parameter, and no operation is performed.
